The objective of this technique is to show how to tag a multi-level (nested) list. The inner list can bee seen as a separate list in a list. The sublist is fully enclosed in an LBody tag. It expresses the relationship with other content of this LBody tag.

Another valid technique for a multi-level list is the hierachical list. The hierachical list can be used for taxonomies or organigrams.
SF_test-s:
Test

Expected Results: 

Checks #⁠1 to #⁠6 are all true. 

Procedures:

  1. Check that all fundamental techniques are followed.
  2. Check that all real content that the author intends as a list is tagged as a list.
  3. Check that list tags are not used when the author does not intend a list.
  4. Check that the substructures of all L tags are semantically appropriate (LI, Lbl, LBody). 
  5. Check that the ListNumbering attribute on all L tags in ordered lists is correctly set to "Decimal", "UpperRoman", "LowerRoman", "UpperAlpha" or "LowerAlpha".
  6. Check that the sublist L tags are within LBody and in the correct logical content order.

WP_Summary: Expansion Text used to describe the lack of content
WP_Description:
The use of expansion text is used here to identify the lack of information. IE Box number. If that box is empty, it states "Box 20 Blank". In this case expansion text is used to explain what number "020" is as well as whether or not it contains a value.

Versus, number "018" which contains a value. In this case, expansion text is used to explain the number 18 only, it states "Box 18".

Each of these elements also contains the French text.

WCAG Success Criterion 3.1.4 (Abbreviations)

As this is a tax form, there are two pieces to this page. The first half is meant for the recipient to keep for their records and the second is to be torn off and sent in with their taxes. 

This content is real content, but is completely duplicated. Therefore the second form was artifacted. There are no specific guidelines or rules for completely duplicated content.

WP_Summary: Use the span tag within one header to identify languages instead of multiple same headers (IE H1, H1)
WP_Description:
For bilingual documents, content is repeated for the languages (Year/Année). You then need to use the same header twice for each language <H1>Year, <H1>Année. There is no explicit rule for using the same header twice instead of using the span tag to merge the two language elements in one header tag. <H1>   <Span>Year -> Language en-ca   <Span>Année -> Language fr-ca   <Span>2014 T4A -> Language en-ca   Versus <H2>Statement of Pension, Retirement, Annuity,and Other Income -> Language en-ca <H2>État du revenu de pension, de retraite, de rente ou d'autres sources -> Language fr-ca

  • or - <H2>   <Span>Statement of Pension, Retirement, Annuity,and Other Income -> Language en-ca   <Span>État du revenu de pension, de retraite, de rente ou d'autres sources -> Language fr-ca

Correspondence can take the form of a covering letter followed by subsequent letters that are copy of letters sent to another addressees. Consider the example of an insurance agent receiving copies of renewal notices sent to their clients. Each letter is tagged as H1 for accessibility with paragraphs and other tags to describe the content of each document

Description:

The objective of this technique is to show the correct use of Article tags to identify, programmatically, individual articles in a document. This could help AT users to navigate in and through articles in a document.

In this example, in the PDF, there are two articles, both of which are properly tagged and the tags for the content of each article are nested inside their own Article tag. 

What is exemplified in this document?

Each article is tagged with a separate article tag.

Rule / Correct Use

Each article must be tagged with a separate article tag. "Fail" example: #27
SF_test-s:
Tests: 

  1. Check that each article, and any necessary sub-structures, based on the content and in accordance with containment rules, are nested in their own Article tag .
  2. Check that the Article tag contains all of the content in the article. 

Expected Results:

Checks #⁠1 and #⁠2 are true.

SF_test-s:
Tests:

  2. Check that the Article tag contains all of the content in the article. 

Expected Results:

Checks #⁠1 or #⁠2 is false.

  6. Check that the first Hn tag, if any, is an H1.

WP_Summary: Heading with a level higher than 6 (rolemapped to P)
WP_Description:
The objective of this technique is to show how to tag headings with a heading level higher than 6. Use a custom Hn tag that expresses the appropriate heading level. Role mapping an Hn heading tag higher than H6 to P could serve the purpose of simplifying the headings hierarchy. Role mapping an Hn heading tag higher than H6 to H6 allows assistive technology that does not support headings above H6 to nonetheless perceive a heading. Choosing an appropriate role mapping is at the discretion of the author. This example shows H7 rolemapped to P. #203 shows H7 rolemapped to H6.

Note: Naming custom heading tags requires the use of the letter H followed by Arabic numerals without any spaces or punctuation. For example, H1247 is permissible while H1,247 and H1 247 are not.
SF_test-s:
Test

Expected Results

Checks #⁠1 to #⁠8 are all true.

Procedure

  1. Check that all fundamental techniques are followed.
  2. Check that all perceivable headings have been tagged as headings.
  3. Check that heading tags are not used when content is not a heading.
  4. Check that either only H tags with appropriate nesting or only Hn tags are used.
  5. Check that the heading levels are appropriate to the content hierarchy of the document.
  6. Check that the first heading, if any, is tagged as an H1.
  7. Check that no heading levels are skipped.
  8. Check that headings with heading levels higher than 6 use custom Hn tags role mapped appropriately.
TX_structure-type: H1-H6 (1.x - Headings with specified levels),H7+ (PDF/UA-1 - Headings with specified levels)

Document divided into 3 parts: front cover, main content, back cover. Only the first part and the last part are marked by a Part structure element.

Part is for a large scale division of a document. Therefore, if you use Part, every content should be inside a Part structure element.

The objective of this technique is to show the correct use of the Part [structure element](https://www.pdfa.org/glossary-of-accessibility-terminology-in-pdf/#structure element). Examples could include:

This describes the failure condition that results when Test #⁠1 is false (a Document tag is not the root element in the structure tree).  In this example, a Sect tag is used at the root level, not a Document tag.  The Document tag as the first, and only, element at the root level in the structure tree is required by the PDF (ISO 32000) and PDF/UA (ISO 14289) specifications.  PDF is similar to a markup language, like HTML for example, and, as such, elements need to be marked (or tagged) at the structure level for accessibility.   

Success Example:  #52

WP_Summary: 1.4.5 Images of text and conformance | Images of text - infographic
WP_Description:
1.4.5 Images of text  and 'conforming alternate version'

 

A pdf only contains an infograhpic. There are two ways to make this accessible:

  1. ALL the information is available in structured text in the pdf itself; There is not just a short label in the alternative text
  2. There is a 'conforming alternate version' that:

 

  • conforms at the designated level, and
  • provides all of the same information and functionality in the same human language, and
  • is as up to date as the non-conforming content, and
  • for which at least one of the following is true:
    1. the conforming version can be reached from the non-conforming page via an accessibility-supported mechanism, or
    2. the non-conforming version can only be reached from the conforming version, or
    3. the non-conforming version can only be reached from a conforming page that also provides a mechanism to reach the conforming version

WP_Description:
Objective: to identify a correct way to create a form field? Is this properly tagged?

I created a simple example: text (First name) and a text field.

The objective of this technique is to show how to tag non-text content that is intended to be consumed as text. In this example, graphics were used to place the word "paragraph" on the page, instead of text objects. To make the characters of this graphic extractable, ActualText on a separate Span tag is used. The ActualText matches the visual appearance of the related graphics.

Note:  The Span tag is always the recommended choice if ActualText is required because ActualText removes the semantics of tags. In spite of the previous statement, ActualText may also work when the tag is one of the following: Lbl (outside of List structures), P, BibEntry, BlockQuote, Caption, Code, Note, Quote.  However, ActualText should never be assigned to other tags.

Note: This passes PDF/UA requirements but not WCAG 1.4.5. Images of text are not allowed in WCAG.

WP_Summary: Heading with a level higher than 6 (rolemapped to H6)
WP_Description:
The objective of this technique is to show how to tag headings with a heading level higher than 6. Use a custom Hn tag that expresses the appropriate heading level. Role mapping an Hn heading tag higher than H6 to H6 allows assistive technology that does not support headings above H6 to nonetheless perceive a heading. Role mapping an Hn heading tag higher than H6 to P could serve the purpose of simplifying the headings hierarchy. Choosing an appropriate role mapping is at the discretion of the author. This example shows H7 rolemapped to H6. #73 shows H7 rolemapped to P.

Note: Naming custom heading tags requires the use of the letter H followed by Arabic numerals without any spaces or punctuation. For example, H1247 is permissible while H1,247 and H1 247 are not.

  5. Check that the heading levels are appropriate to the content hierarchy of the document.
  6. Check that the first heading, if any, is tagged as an H1.
  7. Check that no heading levels are skipped.
  8. Check that headings with heading levels higher than 6 use custom Hn tags role mapped appropriately.

WP_Description:
Description 

The objective of this technique is to show how to mark a title and a subtitle to separate them from the document's heading hierarchy. In this example, the title of the document is in a Title tag, role mapped to P, and the subtitle of the document is in a custom "Subtitle" tag, role mapped to P as well.
SF_test-s:
Test

Expected Results

Checks #⁠1 to #⁠12 are all true.

Procedures

  1. Check that all fundamental techniques are followed.
  2. Check that all perceivable headings have been tagged as headings.
  3. Check that heading tags are not used when content is not a heading.
  4. Check that either only H tags with appropriate nesting or only Hn tags are used.
  5. Check that the heading levels are appropriate to the content hierarchy of the document.
  6. Check that the first heading, if any, is tagged as an H1.
  7. Check that no heading levels are skipped.
  8. Check that headings with heading levels higher than 6 use custom Hn tags role mapped appropriately.
  9. Check that the title of the document has been tagged in a Title tag.
  10. Check that the Title tag is role mapped to P. 
  11. Check that the subtitle of the document has been tagged.
  12. Check that the subtitle's tag is P or is role mapped to P.
TX_structure-type:
H (PDF/UA-1 - Heading with level deduced from tag structure
),H1-H6 (1.x - Headings with specified levels)

WP_Summary: Figure with incorrect placement attribute | Fail
WP_Description: This example demonstrates the failure condition that applies for an image that conveys information, but the placement attribute is incorrect. In this case the Figure [structure element](https://www.pdfa.org/glossary-of-accessibility-terminology-in-pdf/#structure element) is used as a Block element. The placement attribute, however, is inline.

Test

Procedure

Expected Results

WP_Summary: Figure without visible text, but with Alt text and ActualText | Fail
WP_Description: This example demonstrates the failure condition that applies when an image has both ActualText and alt text set. Only the use of alt text, however, is appropriate in this example because the image conveys information and neither contains essential visible text nor represents essential text.
SF_test-s:
Test

Procedure

  1. Check that any decorative image is not tagged with Figure, but is marked as an artifact.
  2. Check that only non-text real content is tagged as Figure.
  3. Check that the Figure tag includes an appropriate alt text.
  4. Check that no ActualText is set, as long as the Figure's content doesn't contain text essential to understanding the Figure's content. 

Expected Results

Checks #⁠1 to #⁠3 are true, check #⁠4 is false.

WP_Summary: ActualText provides correct extractable characters in place of OCR errors
WP_Description:
The objective of this technique is to show how to handle scanned text with OCR. Having invisible text is the prerequisite for tagging the text content and making it machine-readable. Because the invisible text doesn't match its visual appearance, ActualText is necessary. The ActualText is set on the tag level on a separate Span tag. The image of the scanned text is an artifact.

 

Note:  Span is always the recommended choice if ActualText is required because ActualText removes the semantics of tags. In spite of the previous statement, ActualText may also work when the tag is one of the following: Lbl (outside of List structures), P, BibEntry, BlockQuote, Caption, Code, Note, Quote.  However, ActualText should never be assigned to other tags.

Test

Procedure

  2. Check that Unicode can be derived for any text content.
  3. Check that the extractable characters match their visual appearance.
  4. Check that the Natural Language is set for any text content.
  5. If invisible text is used, check that the related images are artifacted.

WP_Summary: Order of marked content sequences within a tag incorrectly set
WP_Description:
This example demonstrates a failure condition in which the order of content within a tag does not match the author’s intent.

Often, when text or a page is rotated, the content order within the tags is incorrect, like in this example. Even though English is written left to right, the lines of text within the paragraphs should be ordered from the right side of the page to the left.

Test

Expected Results

Although check #⁠1 is true, check #⁠2 is false.

Procedures

  1. Check that the logical content order of the tags matches the author’s intent.
  2. Check that the order of the content within each tag matches the author’s intent.

Procedures

  1. Check that all fundamental techniques are followed.
  2. Check that all real content that the author intends as a list is tagged as a list.
  3. Check that list tags are not used when the author does not intend a list.
  4. Check that the substructures of all L tags are semantically appropriate (LI, Lbl, LBody). 
  5. Check that the ListNumbering attribute on all L tags in ordered lists is correctly set to "Decimal", "UpperRoman", "LowerRoman", "UpperAlpha" or "LowerAlpha".
  6. For those list items that include nested lists, check that the nested list's L tag is placed inside the LBody of its containing list item.

WP_Description: The objective of this technique is to show how to tag a Nested list.   In general, the author decides which technique he wants to choose for expressing the semantic relationship of the elements in a list with more than one level. According to the tag matrix in ISO 32000, L tags can be direct childs of another L tag or of an LBody tag. Both techniques are valid, but they express a slightly different semantic relationship between the elements.

Expected Results: 

Checks #⁠1 to #⁠6 are all true. 

Procedures:

  1. Check that all fundamental techniques are followed.
  2. Check that all real content that the author intends as a list is tagged as a list.
  3. Check that list tags are not used when the author does not intend a list.
  4. Check that the substructures of all L tags are semantically appropriate (LI, Lbl, LBody). 
  5. Check that the ListNumbering attribute on all L tags in ordered lists is correctly set to "Decimal", "UpperRoman", "LowerRoman", "UpperAlpha" or "LowerAlpha".
  6. Check that the sublevel L tags are located as direct childs of their corresponding L tag in the correct content order.
  7. For those list items that include nested lists, check that the nested list's L tag is placed inside the LBody of its containing list item.

WP_Description: The objective of this technique is to show how to tag a hierarchical list. This expresses a semantic relationship of a tree view: all elements are bound together into one hierarchical list. It emphasizes that all elements are different branches of this one tree. This can be used for taxonomies or organigrams. For all other nested lists the technique of Multi-level list should be used.

WP_Summary: Linked table of contents correctly tagged
WP_Description:
The objective of this technique is to show how to tag a linked table of contents. In this example Link tags are placed in Reference tags. This is the recommended way of tagging a table of contents.

Alternatively, the link could go into a P tag to avoid currently existing troubles with AT which would read “link, link” when a Link tag is a child of a Reference tag.

Note: The semantically appropriate internal structure of the Link tag is displayed in more detail in the separate use case Link.

In addition, the dot leaders are marked as artifacts because they are created as part of the layout process, and are therefore non-semantic (see Fundamental 3).

WP_Summary: Hierarchical table of contents correctly tagged
WP_Description:
The objective of this technique is to show ho to tag a hierarchical table of contents. In this example, the sublevel TOC is on the same level like the TOCI it is logically situated in. Current assistive technology in some cases can profit from this to announce a nested list to the reader.

The substructure of the TOCI tag follows the recommendation of the default example of use case table of contents, #267.

In addition, the dot leaders are marked as artifacts because they are created as part of the layout process, and are therefore non-semantic (see Fundamental 3).

WP_Description:
The objective of this technique is to show how to tag an internal link.

An internal link destination should be an XYZ destination with zoom unspecified. This generally conveys the required information, so that assistive technology is able to set the focus to the correct target element. This example uses a direct destination. Named destinations would be valid as well.
SF_test-s:
Test

Expected Results: 

Checks #⁠1 to #⁠7 are all true. 

Procedure

  1. Check that all fundamental techniques are followed.
  2. Check that all real content that the author intends as an external link is tagged as a Link.
  3. Check that Link tags are not used when the author does not intend real content to be a link.
  4. Check that the Link tag includes the Link annotations via OBJR element(s).
  5. Check that the Contents entry on the Link annotation is sufficiently informative.
  6. Check that the size and the position of the Link annotation is consistent with its appearance.
  7. Check that the destination of the Link annotation matches the author's intent.
